observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to exhaust all means to locate and validate the claimants of the unpaid obligations in the amount of ₱3,807,196.36 which remained outstanding for more than two years and once claimants and existence of liability could no longer be verified, a report thereon shall be provided to the Municipal Mayor that will be presented to the Sangguniang Bayan which will in turn be the basis of the latter to pass a resolution recommending the dropping of these obligations from the books in order to mobilize public funds to achieve effective resource planning and allocation for the implementation of more projects and service deliveries to the constituents.

2.4.1	to take up the following adjusting entry in the General Fund books as follows:
2.4.2	that henceforth, be guided with the illustrated Pro-forma Accounting Entries for Trust Fund prescribed in Section 104, Volume I, Chapter V of the Manual on NGAS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify all the tangible items that have met the definition and recognition criteria of PPE but with a cost below Fifty Thousand Pesos (₱50,000.00) to the appropriate semi-expendable property or expense account as prescribed in COA Circular 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024, in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements.

To correct the aforementioned deficiencies, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
5.13.1	prepare the following correcting entry:
5.13.2 	cause the immediate remittance of taxes amounting to P123,203.05 with the accountable officer paying for the surcharges, interest and penalties; and see to it that henceforth, all the taxes withheld are remitted within the prescribed period to avoid incurrence of penalties, which could not be charged against government fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the proposed adjusting journal entry to recognize the shares of the DPWH and OBO from collections of Building Permit Fees:

We also recommended that the Municipality create and maintain a Trust Fund Account for Building Permit Fees and other charges as required under Item 2.1 of the DILG and DPWH JMC No. 1 dated July 4, 2014 and henceforth remit the corresponding shares of the DPWH and OBO.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to be guided by the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs, specifically in the recording of the granting of cash advances under the Trust Fund and in the liquidation of the same, to institute proper monitoring of the status of cash advances in the books of accounts and avoid loss of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned employees to immediately submit duly certified record/report proving the distribution of the procured goods to the intended recipients to the Municipal Accountant, who will subsequently submit the same to the Office of the Auditor to ascertain their completeness and propriety and to ensure that these were actually received by the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
10.7.1 	the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the cash allocated for Trust Liability – DRRMF from the bank account of General Fund to the bank account of Trust Fund to facilitate accounting and monitoring of said fund and avoid confusion on the usage of check booklet; and
10.7.2 	the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries after the bank transfer to reflect the correct balances of Cash in Bank for each fund and minimize the balances of the Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant that recoupment of advance payment made shall be deducted proportionately from the contractor’s periodic progress payment/s until the advance is fully liquidated within the duration of the contract, as required in Item 4.3 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, now Section 71.2.7 of IRR of RA No. 12009, otherwise known as the New Government Procurement Act, to avoid the possible loss of government funds in case of default or non-compliance of the contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
13.10.1 	Instruct the Municipal Accountant to adequately observe the deadline set for the submission of trial balances and other financial reports as required under Paragraph 1, Section 122 of PD 1445 and Section 7.2.1a of COA Circular 2009-006;
13.10.2 	Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the revenue and disbursement documents to the Audit Team until the 10th day of the following month,
13.10.3 Cause the suspension of payment of the salaries of the employees responsible for the delayed submission or non-submission of the required reports until the same shall have been complied in pursuance with Paragraph 2 of Section 122 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
14.8.1 	the Municipal Treasurer to see to it that the renewal of fidelity bond of all the Accountable Officers is made before or 30 days from the expiration of the same, as required in Section 4.8 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019, to have continuous protection against potential financial losses; and
14.8.2 	the Municipal Accountant to ensure the sufficiency of the fidelity bond coverage and require the application for additional fidelity bond premium, if needed, based on Annex C of the same circular to guaranty full recovery in cases of cash shortages, defalcations, or unrelieved losses in the accounts of the AOs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain separate cashbook for the RA No. 7171 Fund Current Account and High-Yield Savings Account as required in Section 27, Volume II of the Manuals on NGAS to simplify the bank reconciliation process. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare bank reconciliation statement for each bank account maintained by the municipality to have a better control and monitoring of cash in bank balances to avoid possible incurrence of bank charges for issuing checks with insufficient funds.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan   consider the passage of a resolution authorizing the Municipal Mayor to execute a deed of donation of the PPE accounts currently used by the DepEd to fairly present the balances of PPE and Government Equity accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to ensure that signing of the contract is within ten (10) calendar days from receipt by the winning bidder of the NOA in adherence to Section 37.2.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA 9184 in order to avoid the incurrence of delay in the procurement process and in the implementation of the contract.

The Notice of Award (NOA) of 43 procurements for infrastructures and goods with contract cost aggregating ₱223,397,847.90 were not posted in the PhilGEPS website within the reglementary period prescribed in Sections 37.1.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184, thus the objectives of the procurement law to promote transparency and efficiency in the conduct of procurement in the government was not served. 

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC Secretariat to post the NOA in the PhilGEPs on the prescribed period as required in Sections 37.1.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184, in order to conform with the objectives of the procurement law in promoting transparency and efficiency in the conduct of procurement activities.

Likewise, to immediately submit the proof or printouts on the posting of award of the abovementioned projects to the Auditor through the Municipal Accountant to determine compliance to the posting requirement set forth in Section 37.1.6 of the same Act.

The Net Financial Contracting Capacity (NFCC) of the four contracts for infrastructure projects aggregating ₱14,284,648.56 were not accurately computed thus, the basis for verifying the liquidity and capacity of the bidder to absorb additional obligations is affected.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to carefully verify/check the computation of submitted NFCC to ensure that the bidder has the capacity to finance and implement the bid contract.

The list of contractor’s major equipment units pledged to six infrastructure projects aggregating ₱29,318,903.64 did not meet the minimum requirement specified in the Bid Data Sheet (BDS), thus there is no certainty that the contractor can undertake the construction of these projects.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to review carefully the submitted documents ensuring that the contractor’s major equipment units pledged qualify with the minimum requirement specified in the BDS, hence to guarantee the ability of the bidder to undertake and complete the contract.

The Performance Security of the Improvement of Post-Harvest Facility at Bidbiday project with contract cost of ₱1,796,258.20 was posted after the date of the perfection of the contract, contrary to Sections 37.2.1, 37.2.3 and 39.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184, manifesting that Management entered into contract without requiring the completion of relevant documents forming part of the contract, exposing the same to possible risk of non-performance of obligation by the contractor.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to ensure that the Performance Security are duly/properly dated and posted prior to the perfection of the contract in compliance with Sections 37.2.1, 37.2.3 and 39.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of 9184 to guarantee the faithful performance by the winning bidder of its obligations.

12.3.7.2 	Scrutinize the eligibility documents of all the interested bidders by applying the non-discretionary “pass/fail” criterion and recommend award of the contract only to the eligible bidder who has the lowest calculated responsive bid.
12.4	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to ensure that signing of the contract is within ten (10) calendar days from receipt by the winning bidder of the Notice of Award (NOA) in adherence to Section 37.2.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA No. 9184 in order to avoid the incurrence of delay in the procurement process and in the implementation of the contract.
12.5	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC Secretariat to post the NOA in the PhilGEPS within three days from issuance as required in Sections 37.1.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, to conform to the objectives of the procurement law in promoting transparency and efficiency in the conduct of procurement activities.

13.4	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to:
13.4.1 	stop the practice of splitting contracts  to avoid public bidding;
13.4.2 	conduct competitive public bidding for all procurements and resort to alternative methods of procurement only when the conditions provided in the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 are met; and
13.4.3 	see to it that the most advantageous price is obtained in every procurement.

14.7	We recommended and Management agreed to inform the Auditor in writing on the posting of infrastructure project billboards within ten days after the award of the contract and comply with the submission of monthly reports of on-going and completed contracts as required under COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 20, 2013, to promote transparency in the implementation of infrastructure projects and to ensure that the right of the people to information on matters of public concern is addressed.

15.4	We recommended that that the Municipal Mayor require the GSO Designate to notify the Audit Team of all deliveries of procured goods within twenty-four (24) hours from the acceptance so that the Auditor could conduct timely inspection/validation of the same and communicate the results thereof for Management’s consideration.

16.5	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
16.5.1 	the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the cash allocated for Trust Liability – DRRMF from the bank account of General Fund to the bank account of Trust Fund to facilitate accounting and monitoring  of  said fund and avoid confusion on the usage of check booklet to be used; and
16.5.2 	the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries after the bank transfer to reflect the correct balances of Cash in Bank for each fund  and minimize  the balances of the Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds accounts.

17.6	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
17.6.1 	the Municipal Engineer to instruct all contractors to see to it that all laborers are provided with complete and appropriate PPEs and cause for the installation of warning devices at the project site whenever necessary in order not to compromise the health and safety of the workers and the public, and henceforth indicate the actual percentage of completion of the pay items in the SWA; and
17.6.2	the Municipal Accountant to carefully scrutinize the supporting documents of  disbursement vouchers and determine with certainty the amount  due to the contractors in order not to unduly benefit the latter at the expense of the municipality.

18.8	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit collections intact with the authorized depository bank whenever  the latter goes to the bank for the encashment of check/s  or when collections reach ₱10,000.00 to avoid the accumulation of huge cash  on hand, thereby minimizing the risk of possible loss, misuse or misappropriation of government funds and at the same time enable the municipality to earn additional income in the form of interest on bank deposits.

19.5	We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct all officials and employees to submit liquidation documents and refund the excess cash advance if any, when the purpose of the cash advance has been served and to liquidate all cash advances at year-end.

20.7	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary documents for the application of bond for the GSO Designate and pay the corresponding bond premiums to protect the interest of the government.

21.6	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant that henceforth ensure that all revenue and disbursement documents including the corresponding journals shall be submitted to the Commission on Audit not later than the 10th day of the following month to permit timely post audit of these transactions so that deficiencies, if any, could be communicated to management on time for appropriate corrective action.

22.6.1 	instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all JEVs are duly supported with complete documents including explanations/computations to warrant easy verification on the validity and propriety of the transactions including the correctness of  accounting entries made, and
22.6.2	require the  Municipal Accountant to follow the prescribed procedures in the recording of  financial transactions from the JEVs to  the corresponding journals then to  the General Ledgers as embodied in Annexes 1 to 5 of Volume II  of the MNGAS to ensure systematic verification of accounts presented in the financial statements.

23.7	We recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to submit the quarterly financial statements on time to maximize the usefulness of the financial information and enable Management to make timely corrective measures for any errors, deficiencies or irregularities discovered through the financial statements.

24.4	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, furnish the same to the LDRRMO for the latter to reconcile with his record and submit to the COA Auditor not later than the 15th day after the end of each month so that errors/deficiencies could be communicated to Management on time for appropriate corrective action.

25.8	In order to address the aforementioned deficiencies, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
25.8.1 	consider the revision of the Contract of Lease by including among others the rate  of rental fee per month, area of the space being rented out and penalty/surcharge on late payments, and
25.8.2 	instruct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly enforce the terms and conditions of the Contract of Lease and  send demand letter/s to the lessees to remind them of their obligations to pay.

26.8	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
26.8.1 	require the one in-charge in the issuance of Driver’s Trip Tickets to put serial number in chronological order on all Driver’s Trip Tickets and see to it that the same are approved by the authorized official before these are issued to the drivers;
26.8.2 	instruct all drivers   not to drive municipal vehicles/trucks/ heavy equipment without duly approved Driver’s Trip Ticket and    fill out the corresponding data required therein before and after the trip, and
26.8.3 	require the Municipal Treasurer who acts as the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to assist the drivers in the preparation of the Monthly Report of Official Travels    and instruct them to prepare the Report of Fuel Consumption for all vehicles/heavy equipment owned by the municipality and submit the same to the Auditor within the first ten days of the succeeding month.

27.6	We recommended that the  “Requesting Official”  portion of the CAFOA and Box A of the disbursement voucher of Special Education Fund be signed by the district supervisor or his/her authorized representative to enable the said official to monitor disbursements and accomplishments and most importantly, to institute corrective/remedial measures to ensure full attainment of targets.

28.4 We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to fill out all the data indicated in the CAFOA to ascertain that the expenditures being obligated are within the budget and are charged to the proper office, function/program/project and account code to avoid the incurrence of overdraft in appropriation and facilitate verification of the Statement of Comparison of Budget versus Actual Amounts.

29.3	We recommend that henceforth, the Municipal Mayor:
29.3.1 	instruct the Municipal Engineer to require the contractors to turn over the scaffoldings to the municipality which would help reduce the cost of  future construction projects of the municipality; and
29.3.2 	consider the programming of rental of scaffoldings in the Program of Work for future vertical structure infrastructure projects instead of  procurement of scaffoldings to lessen the cost of the projects.
30.6	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause for the immediate use of the two buildings to generate additional revenue in the form of rental and market fees.

31.4	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer:
31.4.1 	intensify the collection of real property taxes and conduct massive tax campaign in all the barangays; and
31.4.2 	seek the cooperation of barangay officials since they have also shares  on the collection of real property taxes.

32.5	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Point System to submit the GAD Plan and Budget to the COA Audit Team within five days from receipt of the DILG-endorsed GPB, and the GAD Accomplishment Report duly received by the DILG within five days from the end of January of the ensuing year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the abovementioned provision of BIR Revenue Regulation No. 2 -98 by consistently deducting taxes due from payments to suppliers and remit the same to the BIR within the prescribed period, thus the National Government would have additional funds for its operations, programs and projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant
to trace the discrepancy between the balances of the accounts
RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred RPT/SET and effect the necessary
adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the correct balances in the
financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry to correct the
balance of affected accounts by debiting Special Education Tax
Receivable and crediting Deferred Special Education Fund Tax by
the amount of P909,873.61 in order to reflect the correct balance of
these accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Accountant to review available records in order to pinpoint what year these
inventories were recorded and results/details of which will be verified with the
Property Officer and concerned department heads for
clarification/reconciliation, then prepare the necessary adjusting journal
entry in the books of accounts to restate the balances of Inventory and
Government Equity accounts in order to reflect fairly the balances in the
financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant to:

4.6.1 prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to reclassify and
record the properties in their appropriate accounts by debiting
Road Networks by the amount of 12,975,272.41 and crediting
Buildings, CIP - Infra Assets and Other Infra Assets were
overstated by the amounts of P7,990,604.87, P1,362,389.63 and
3,622,277.91 respectively; and

4.6.2 verify the records on the details of projects included in the CIP-
Infrastructure Assets account and those that were transferred to
their appropriate accounts to locate for the discrepancy amounting
to P14,139,126.70, thus reflecting the correct balances of the
affected accounts, thereby the financial statements is accurately and
fairly presented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory
Committee to conduct physical annual inventory as required under Section
156 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs to
ascertain, as well as to provide basis on the validity, existence,
serviceability and correctness of the properties presented in the financial
statement.

Likewise, instruct the Property Officer, the Head of Inventory Committee and the
Municipal Accountant to adhere with the guidelines and procedures set forth
under COA Circular 2020-06 dated January 31, 2020 for the conduct of physical
count of properties; recognition of PPE items found at station; disposition for
non-existing/missing PPE items; and for the one-time cleansing of PPE
account balances, in order to reflect more reliable and accurate balances of PPE
accounts, hence to convey precise information useful in decision making and
accountability for these properties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

6.7.1 Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to be keen in observing the
validity period of the contractor’s Authority to Print (ATP)
covering the official receipts issued in order that payments would
be properly acknowledged with valid official receipts and to prevent
the repetition of same condition as discussed in the foregoing; and
6.7.2 Instruct the Municipal Accountant to review carefully the validity
of supporting documents of vouchers prior to their submission to
the audit team, hence, validity and propriety of the transactions are
ascertained.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Accountant strict compliance of the abovementioned regulations
pertaining to the submission of the monthly and quarterly trial balances,
including the supporting SAOB, journals, ledgers and supporting
schedules to facilitate review and verification thereon, and deficiencies if
any, may be immediately communicated to management, for the latter’s
consideration.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

8.7.1 instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare immediately the
disbursement vouchers and supporting schedules for the remittance of
RPT/CTC shares and municipal aid to the concerned LGUs to avoid
possible misappropriation/misuse of the funds and to prevent these funds
from dormancy; and

8.7.2 instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to remit
promptly/regularly the shares of the Province and Barangays from the
RPT/CTC collections and municipal aid in adherence to the provision of
Section 271 (d) and 164 (c) of RA 7160, thus helping them finance their
operations and development projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to strictly adhere to the abovementioned provision of BIR
Revenue Regulation No. 2 -98 by consistently deducting taxes due from
payments to suppliers and remit the same to the BIR within the prescribed
period, thus the National Government would have additional funds for its
operations, programs and projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Budget Officer to strictly observe the 55% limitation in the appropriation
for personal services as required under Section 325 (a) of the Local
Government Code in the succeeding years, hence other funds will be
allocated for other beneficial programs and development projects of the
Municipality. Subsequent expenditures incurred in excess of the PS limit
will be dealt with audit disallowance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Engineer to submit immediately said lacking documents to the Audit
Team through the Municipal Accountant in order to verify the validity
and propriety of these transactions and to facilitate immediate request for
inspection thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
12.7.1 Require all Department Heads strict compliance with Section 4 (6)
of PD 1445 and pertinent provisions of COA Circular 2012-001
dated June 14, 2012 and the Municipal Accountant to meticulously
scrutinize all disbursement vouchers submitted for processing and
ensure that these are completely supported with the required
documents before effecting payment thereof to ascertain the
validity, legality, propriety and correctness of the financial
transactions; and
12.7.2 As regards the foregoing transactions, instruct the Municipal
Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking
documents and address the deficiencies noted to determine the
validity, propriety and correctness of these transactions and in
compliance with the abovementioned provisions of law and
regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to notify and bill these contractors/suppliers on the additional
taxes due them and to impose the collection thereof.

13.9 Likewise, strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 2A.02 (b) and (e)
of the Municipal Ordinance No. 30-2021 or the New Revenue Code of the
Municipality on the imposition of business taxes in the succeeding years,
in order to collect additional revenue for the operations and development
projects of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to intensify the collection of Real Property and Special
Education Taxes. Send notices of collection to the delinquent taxpayers
to remind them of their obligation. Likewise, conduct massive tax
campaign in all the barangays and offer tax amnesty in order to motivate
delinquent taxpayers to avail of the offer.

15.1.1.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to carefully verify/check the computation of
submitted NFCC to ensure that the bidder has the
capacity to finance and implement the bid contract.

15.1.2.3 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
BAC to ensure that the Performance Security are

duly/properly dated and posted prior to contract signing
in compliance with Sections 37.2.1, 37.2.3 and 39.1 of the
2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to guarantee the
faithful performance by the winning bidder of its
obligations.

15.1.3.3 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
BAC to review carefully the submitted documents
ensuring that the contractor’s major equipment units
pledged qualify with the minimum requirement specified
in the BDS, hence to guarantee the ability of the bidder
to undertake and complete the contract.

15.1.4.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
BAC Secretariat to post the NOA in the PhilGEPs with
in the prescribed period as required in Sections 37.1.6 of
the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, in order to
conform with the objectives of the procurement law in
promoting transparency and efficiency in the conduct of
procurement activities.

15.1.4.7 Likewise, to immediately submit the proof or printouts
on the posting of award of the abovementioned projects
to the Auditor through the Municipal Accountant to
determine compliance to the posting requirement set
forth in Section 37.1.6 of the same Act.

15.1.5.8 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive
instruct the BAC strict compliance with the provisions
set forth in Items 5.2 and 5.3 of DOLE DO No. 13, series
of 1998, Section III.A of DPWH DO No. 56, series of 2005
and Sections 37.2.2 and 37.2.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of
RA 9184 by requiring the submission of CSHP duly
approved by the DOLE prior to the approval of the
contract, to ensure the safety and protection of the
workers employed as well as the general public.

15.1.5.9 Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to deduct
the cost of CSHP included in the contract cost as these
were not employed during construction.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Budget Officer to facilitate the allocation of one percent (1%) of the IRA
for the program/projects/ activities of LCPC intended for the welfare and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct Inventory Committee and Municipal Accountant exert their best efforts to locate the foregoing discrepancies and cause the reconciliation of the balances of PPE accounts in the ledgers with that of the RPCPPE as basis for the preparation of adjusting journal entry, if necessary, to reflect the correct balances.
Likewise, require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entry voucher to reclassify the motor vehicles and ICT equipment to their proper accounts to present fairly these PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to include in the conduct of annual physical count of properties all unissued inventory items of the LGU and prepare the RPCI to determine the existence and serviceability of the same.
Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee to periodically reconcile the balances of inventory accounts in the ledgers with that of the RCPI as basis for the preparation of adjusting journal entry, if necessary, to reflect fairly the balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant exert extra effort to locate the source documents and MOA or Trust Agreements with the source agencies and review the terms and conditions relative to the implementation of programs and projects and determine the viability of the usage of funds and if the same could still be used for other projects, seek the approval of the source agencies, if deemed necessary.
Moreover, instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer return the unexpended/unutilized balances of completed projects to the source agencies, thereby allowing them to use the same for other priority projects of the government.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to locate or trace the documents of recorded infrastructure assets and make the necessary adjustments in the books to reflect the correct financial condition and performance of the LGU in the financial statements.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor:
Require all Department Heads strict compliance with Section 4 (6) of PD 1445 and pertinent provisions of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012. The Municipal Accountant to meticulously scrutinize all disbursement vouchers submitted for processing and ensure that these are completely supported with the required documents before effecting payment thereof to ascertain the validity, legality, propriety and correctness of the financial transactions.
As regards the foregoing transactions, instruct the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents and address the deficiencies noted to determine the validity, propriety and correctness of all the transactions and in compliance with the abovementioned provisions of law and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly observe the provisions set forth in Items 2 and 3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Sections 80 and 84 to 86 of the GAM for LGU to avoid possible loss or misuse of government funds;
Refrain from paying transactions in cash, except those provided in the aforesaid provisions of law. Determine the fairness for possible increase of Petty Cash Fund from which small/petty expenses be charged/paid; and
Strictly follow the rules and regulations in granting cash advances and ensure that proper/sound internal control mechanisms over the cash and disbursement process are administered.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that Box F of the disbursement voucher provided for the recording of accounting entries for the transactions are duly and completely filled out to facilitate verification of the correctness and accuracy of accounting entries made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to strictly comply with the provisions set forth in COA Circular No. 2013-004. Accomplish and update signboards/billboards to enable the public to see and read relevant information for them to assess if PPAs are fairly implemented and justifiably completed which in turn promotes good governance through transparency.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC Chairman to immediately submit the approved contracts and supporting documents of the aforementioned projects to the Auditor in order for the latter to verify the validity, propriety and correctness of these transactions and immediate communication of results thereof.
Henceforth, furnish the Auditor a copy of contracts and supporting documents within five (5) working days from date of execution pursuant to Commission on Audit Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2019.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to:
intensify monitoring and evaluation of ongoing projects and strictly observed the calibrated actions in response to delays in the implementation of infrastructure projects provided in Item 7 of Annex E of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184 and Item 4.2 of GPPB Circular No. 03-2019;  and
prepare the corresponding SWA, STEWA and detailed computation supporting the reported unaccomplished portion of the contract and liquidated damages to verify its correctness and accuracy.

We recommended and Management agreed to identify projects and programs which are contributory to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU and shall partake the form of investment or capital expenditures to fully attain the objectives and purposes for the allocation of 20% DF.
Likewise, for future transactions of the same nature, provide budget in the general fund if these are necessary in the efficient discharge of the LGU’s operations and avoid charging the same from the 20% DF.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the LDRRMO prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with the prescribed format under COA Circular No. 2012-002 to show extensively the pertinent information in the utilization of DRRM fund.
Likewise, prepare the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF using the prescribed format and submit the same on or before the 15th day after the end of each   month to the COA Auditor in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 to enable the timely evaluation of the utilization of the fund.

Moreover, we advise management to continue the implementation of the COA Order of Execution (COE) and the instruction provided in the authority to settle through installment payment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management conduct an annual physical inventory of all its properties, provide all the necessary data in the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and the result of the physical inventory of the PPE shall reconcile with the balance in the property cards and any discrepancies should be immediately verified and adjusted.

We recommended that management dispose immediately those assets considered unserviceable to avoid further devaluation of the properties and to strictly follow the process of disposal as prescribed in the COA Circular 92-386 “Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management”. Furthermore, the Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (	IIRUP) shall be used as a basis to record dropping from the books the unserviceable properties carried in the Property, Plant and Equipment and Inventory accounts.

We recommended that management prioritize the processing and transferring of the title of the land in the name of the Municipality to document the legality of ownership of the land. In addition to the deed of absolute sale executed by the property owner and the government, the Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT), Tax Declaration (TD) and Real Property Tax Certificate must be in the name of the procuring entity or annotation of the Deed of Absolute Sale to show proof of ownership over the property or land acquired and make them available anytime for immediate reference in the validation of the recorded Land in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provision stated above. Collections for the day should be deposited intact with the authorized depository bank. The balance of collections not deposited during the day due to cut-off should be deposited in the next working day to avoid risks from using the collection for paying obligations and exposing the funds to risk from loss or theft.

We recommended that the Municipal Government send collection notices and exert all efforts to collect said receivables from the concerned beneficiaries to ensure that loss or wastage of government funds be prevented as provided in Section 2 of PD 1445. To safeguard all the resources of the government, an efficient and effective collection scheme must be adhered to.

We recommended that management through the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the above cited provision. Submit on time all the necessary reports together with their complete supporting documents in order not to delay the post audit of transactions and preparation of other reports. It is the direct responsibility of the agency head to install, implement, and monitor sound internal control.

We recommended that management require the submission of the Summary of Material Tests Performed before the conduct of inspection by the agency and before the payment of the claim for infrastructure projects. The results of the material analysis conducted will help the municipality in the determination of the quality of materials being used. Further actions of the management could be executed for substandard materials used in the infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), through its Secretariat post all the Procurement Opportunities in the (PhilGEPS) website, the website of the Procuring Entity concerned, if available, and at any conspicuous place reserved for this purpose in the premises of the Procuring Entity for a period of at least three (3) calendar days and posting of NOA and Contract/PO within ten (10) days from their issuance as provided in IV-E and L of Annex “H” of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A No. 9184 to promote transparency and efficiency in the procurement process and extend equal opportunity to all the eligible and qualified suppliers.

We recommended that management ensure that the 20% Development Fund be optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic developments and environmental outcomes of the LGU. It should not be used for any purpose beyond those expressly prescribed under the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017. The utilization of the 20% DF, whether wilfully or through negligence, for any purpose other than those expressly prescribed by law or public policy shall be subject to the sanctions provided under RA No. 7160 and other applicable laws.

We recommended that management conduct the necessary field surveys and investigations in the detailed engineering and carefully study/evaluate the site condition in the preparation of accurate design plans, technical specifications, quantity and cost estimates and program of work for infrastructure projects to minimize any work suspensions that may cause delay in the completion of the projects.

We recommended that management should be reminded that employees receiving additional compensation such as commissions, honoraria, fringe benefits, benefits in excess of the allowable statutory amount of P82,000.00 are not exempt from the mandatory Withholding of Income Tax on Compensation and to be remitted on or before the 10th day of the following month in which the withholding was made except for tax returns covering transactions in December which shall be filed on or before January 15 of the succeeding year.

We recommended that management immediately enforce the settlement of the audit suspensions and disallowances as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Moreover, we advise management to continue the implementation of the COA Order of Execution (COE) and the instruction provided in the authority to settle through installment payment.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee of the municipality conduct further verification to reconcile the balance of the Property, Plant and Equipment account as presented in the financial statements with the balance in the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE). In addition, transactions shall be posted promptly from source documents and any discrepancies noted in Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC) with the physical inventory should be immediately verified and adjusted. Furthermore, Transfer Certificate of Titles of the Land must be in the name of the municipality to show proof of ownership.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted the 2017 RPCPPE
observations.reason: The balances of the Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) in the Financial Statements reconciled with the balances appearing in the Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) submitted for CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management comply all the documentary requirements in the purchase of land under Section 13.1 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to avoid doubts on the validity and propriety of the transaction. Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT), Tax Declaration (TD), Real Property Tax Certificate and other documents necessary to transfer the title must be submitted by the owner to the municipality and the latter shall cause the annotation of the deed of absolute sale on the Transfer Certificate of Title. The Certificate of Title must be registered in the name of the Municipality to show valid proof of ownership of the acquired land.

observations.reason: Post-audit of transactions revealed that the LGU paid transfer fee for the purchase of land to Provincial Treasurer’s Office amounting to P37,224.00

The Agency Head shall be held responsible for the proper implementation of the Joint Circular. Any unauthorized or excess payments shall be refunded by the employees concerned.
